globalOpportunities.name: World AI Competition for Youth (WAICY)
globalOpportunities.deadline: Project submission: Jan 20 — Regional finals: Mar–Apr
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: One of the largest AI competitions specifically for young people worldwide. Teams build AI projects addressing real global challenges, compete in regional championships, and present to international panels.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: WAICY is built for your age group and explicitly welcomes high schoolers. Past student projects have addressed wildfire prediction, crop disease detection, and mental health chatbots. The regional structure means you compete first locally before advancing, making it far less intimidating than adult-focused competitions.
tips: Join as a team of 2–4 for best results — combine coding, design, and domain expertise
The judging emphasizes real-world impact over technical complexity
Register early — regional capacity fills up

globalOpportunities.name: International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence (IOAI)
globalOpportunities.eligibility: Age ≤ 20 by July 1, pre-university; selected via national olympiad (USAAIO for US students)
globalOpportunities.deadline: USAAIO selection: Nov–Mar; IOAI: Aug 2–8, 2026 in Astana, Kazakhstan
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: The premier international science olympiad for artificial intelligence, modeled after IMO and IPhO. Students from 60+ countries compete in theoretical AI problems and practical ML tasks. US students qualify through USAAIO (usaaio.org).
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: IOAI is the most prestigious high school AI competition in the world. Even qualifying at the national level (USAAIO) is a top-tier achievement that opens doors to elite university admissions and research internships. The syllabus covers ML theory, algorithms, and data science — all skills that transfer directly to college coursework.
tips: Start preparing with USAAIO past problems: usaaio.org/past-problems
Study linear algebra, probability, and Python ML libraries — the core of the exam
USAAIO registration typically opens in fall — watch their website

globalOpportunities.name: APEX AI High School Championship
globalOpportunities.eligibility: All US high school students; no prerequisites, all experience levels
globalOpportunities.deadline: Early app: Aug; General: Nov; Final: Feb — competitions run spring
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: A national US AI and data science competition with no experience prerequisites, organized by Correlation One (which places thousands of engineers at top companies annually). Focuses on practical problem-solving with real datasets.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: Correlation One has a direct pipeline to internships and jobs at companies like Goldman Sachs, Google, and the US government. Competing — even without winning — puts your name in front of their recruiter network. The beginner-friendly entry point means you don't need a competition background.
tips: Use the early application round — acceptance is easier than later rounds
Practice on Kaggle beginner competitions to prepare
Correlation One offers free prep workshops — attend them

globalOpportunities.name: Google Solution Challenge
globalOpportunities.eligibility: University student teams (high school: prep & observer track)
globalOpportunities.deadline: Applications typically open Aug–Sep; submission deadline Jan–Feb
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: Build an app addressing one or more of the UN's 17 Sustainable Development Goals using Google technology. The world's largest student developer competition with 100K+ participants.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: Partner with a college GDSC chapter to participate as a high school collaborator. Study the winning projects — AI-powered waste sorting, accessible navigation apps, crop disease detection — to understand what impactful AI product development looks like in practice.
tips: Team up with a college GDSC chapter as a collaborator
Pick one SDG and solve a hyper-local version of the problem
Use Gemini API for rapid NLP features without heavy ML infrastructure

globalOpportunities.name: Technovation Girls
globalOpportunities.deadline: Register Oct–Dec; project submission typically April
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: A global non-profit program encouraging girls aged 8–18 to build mobile apps or AI projects that solve real community problems. Includes a business plan component alongside the technical build.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: Teams write a business plan alongside their app, learning both technical and entrepreneurial skills. Past winners tackled climate change, mental health, and food insecurity. No enrollment fee and extensive support resources. One of the strongest college essay topics available to female STEM students.
tips: Focus on a problem the team personally experiences — judges value authenticity
The business plan section is taken as seriously as the app
Reach out to Technovation ambassadors in your region for mentorship

globalOpportunities.name: The Earth Prize
globalOpportunities.deadline: Applications open Nov; deadline typically March
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: A global award for 13–19 year olds using innovative technology to solve environmental sustainability challenges. Prize: up to $100,000 implementation grant — one of the largest awards available to high schoolers.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: One of the few competitions that explicitly targets high schoolers AND offers a six-figure implementation grant rather than a trophy. Teams using AI for remote sensing, satellite data analysis, or predictive environmental modeling consistently perform well. No prior competition experience required.
tips: Pair AI/ML prediction with real environmental datasets (NASA Earthdata, NOAA)
The implementation plan matters — judges want to see how the prize money would be used
Partner with a local environmental organization to validate your problem framing

globalOpportunities.name: Regeneron ISEF — AI & Machine Learning Category
globalOpportunities.eligibility: Grades 9–12 worldwide; qualify through affiliated regional/state fair
globalOpportunities.deadline: Qualify through local science fair (fall–winter); ISEF held each May
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: The world's largest international pre-college science competition with 1,700+ students from 60+ countries. The Robotics & Intelligent Machines and Systems Software categories are ideal for AI projects. Grand Award winners receive $50,000 scholarships.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: ISEF grand award winners are household names at top universities — the competition carries more weight than almost any extracurricular. AI projects have dominated winning categories in recent years, especially in healthcare diagnostics, climate modeling, and accessibility tools. Start at your regional science fair in 9th or 10th grade to build experience.
tips: Find your affiliated regional science fair: societyforscience.org/affiliated-fairs
Research projects (original hypothesis + experimental data) score higher than tool-building projects
Connect with a university professor or lab for mentorship — judges value academic collaboration

globalOpportunities.name: ITU AI for Good Youth Summit
globalOpportunities.eligibility: Youth delegates and student innovators worldwide
globalOpportunities.deadline: Annual event — Geneva (May–June); applications open 3–4 months prior
techStack: Any AI/ML stack relevant to your chosen SDG
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: The United Nations' premier platform for AI innovation with social impact. Youth Summit sessions and student project showcases run alongside the main conference, putting student work in front of UN agency heads and global AI researchers.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: Participating in an ITU AI for Good event — even as a virtual delegate — puts your work in front of international policymakers and global AI researchers. Student projects showcased here have gone on to receive UN accelerator support. The exposure is unmatched.
tips: Submit a project proposal months in advance for the student showcase
Frame your project using official UN SDG language — it helps with evaluation criteria
Virtual participation is often free — follow ITU AI for Good on social media for early announcements

openSource.name: CrowdSource by Google
openSource.desc: A public platform where anyone can contribute to making AI fairer and more inclusive — through image labeling, handwriting recognition, translation validation, and more. Each small contribution helps train AI models that work better for all languages, cultures, and communities.
openSource.whyJoin: The perfect entry point for students with no coding experience who still want to contribute to AI development. Start in minutes, and your contributions directly reduce bias in Google's production AI systems. It's a genuine portfolio talking point: 'I contributed to AI fairness at Google.'

openSource.name: OpenStreetMap × AI Integration (HOT Tasks)
openSource.sdg: SDG 11 — Sustainable Cities & SDG 13 — Climate Action
openSource.desc: Use computer vision tools to automatically identify buildings, roads, and infrastructure from satellite imagery, then contribute human-verified annotations. HOT (Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team) coordinates disaster response mapping missions — your contributions directly support humanitarian aid operations in real crises.
openSource.whyJoin: Directly relevant for computer vision and geospatial AI interests. Past AI Club members used this experience as a foundation for spatial data science fair projects. When a disaster happens (earthquake, flood), HOT activates urgent mapping tasks — your contribution has real-world impact within days.

openSource.name: Hugging Face 'AI for Good' Hub
openSource.desc: A curated collection of open-source models built for social good — wildfire spread prediction, endangered species vocalization recognition, mental health screening tools, medical imaging diagnostics. All models are forkable with documented training code and free GPU compute via Hugging Face Spaces.
openSource.whyJoin: Fork a model that interests you, fine-tune it on local data, and deploy it as a Hugging Face Space. This gives you a live, shareable AI project in your portfolio within days. Recommended starting projects: wildlife detection via CV transfer learning, or NLP classification for social media misinformation.

openSource.name: Common Voice by Mozilla
openSource.desc: Mozilla's open dataset initiative to make voice recognition technology accessible to everyone. Contribute by recording short sentences or validating existing recordings in any language. The dataset is used by researchers and developers worldwide to build speech recognition AI for underrepresented languages.
openSource.whyJoin: Takes 10 minutes to start. If you speak a language other than English, your contributions are especially valuable — Cantonese, Hokkien, Spanish, Armenian, Hmong, and Korean contributions are all needed. This directly addresses linguistic inequality in AI systems.

openSource.name: Agenta — Open-Source LLM App Platform
openSource.desc: An open-source platform for building, evaluating, and deploying LLM applications. Agenta handles prompt management, model evaluation, and observability. The team actively mentors contributors and has 70+ contributors from around the world. Beginner-friendly issues are labeled in GitHub.
openSource.whyJoin: If you want real production-grade AI engineering experience, this is one of the best beginner-to-intermediate open source projects. Maintainers respond to PRs quickly, will review your code, and give feedback. Fix documentation, improve evaluation metrics, or build new integrations — all count.

openSource.name: Ollama — Local LLM Runtime
openSource.desc: A lightweight runtime for running large language models locally on your own computer, without sending data to cloud APIs. Ollama made local AI accessible to millions of developers. It's one of GitHub's most-starred AI projects with active contribution opportunities.
openSource.whyJoin: Ollama touches AI infrastructure, model serving, and systems programming. Contributing — even to documentation, model cards, or issue triage — gives you a line on your resume that signals deep understanding of how LLMs actually work under the hood.

openSource.name: Open WebUI — Self-Hosted AI Interface
openSource.desc: A polished open-source interface for interacting with local and remote AI models. Together with Ollama, it's become the most popular self-hosted AI stack for privacy-conscious developers and organizations. Built with Python (backend) and Svelte (frontend).
openSource.whyJoin: Front-end contributions (UI improvements, accessibility, internationalization) are especially welcomed and don't require ML expertise. If you know React/Svelte or Python, there are consistently labeled 'good first issue' entries. Privacy-preserving AI is a growing field — relevant for healthcare, education, and government.

localAction.intro: Real programs in California where you can volunteer, participate, or build AI skills alongside local communities. All opportunities below are verified and active.

programs.name: California Free AI Training Program
programs.org: California Governor's Office / CSU + Community Colleges
programs.location: Los Angeles, Sacramento, San Francisco (+ online)
programs.desc: Governor Newsom launched a statewide free AI training initiative for high schoolers (9th grade and up) with classes at California State University campuses, community colleges, and select LA/Sacramento/SF high schools. No coding experience required. Online/mobile-friendly options available for rural students.
programs.howToJoin: Contact your school counselor or check with your nearest CSU campus or community college for available sections. Online enrollment is available at californiacolleges.edu.

programs.name: aiEDU — AI Literacy Volunteer Coach
programs.desc: aiEDU is a nonprofit dedicated to AI literacy in K-12 schools, with a focus on under-resourced communities in LA. They recruit volunteer AI coaches — high school and college students who help younger students learn about AI tools, ethics, and careers. No teaching degree required.
programs.howToJoin: Apply at aiedu.org/volunteer. Volunteers complete a 2-hour online training module, then commit to 1–2 sessions per month at a partner school. Strong recommendation letter potential.

programs.name: Inspirit AI — Student Program (LA/Irvine)
programs.org: Inspirit AI (founded by Stanford & MIT students)
programs.location: Online + in-person at UCLA, Stanford, MIT
programs.desc: A live online and in-person AI program taught by Stanford and MIT students, designed specifically for high schoolers. Students build and deploy real AI models (image classification, NLP, reinforcement learning) over 10 sessions. Scholarships available for under-resourced students.
programs.howToJoin: Apply at inspiritai.com. Cohorts run after school, on weekends, and in summer. Financial aid covers up to 100% of the cost for qualifying students.
programs.timeline: Multiple cohorts per year — summer cohorts are largest

programs.name: LA Works — Tech for Good Volunteer
programs.desc: LA Works is Los Angeles's largest volunteer organization, coordinating thousands of volunteers with nonprofits across the county. Their Tech for Good program specifically matches students with AI/tech skills to nonprofits that need help with data analysis, chatbot setup, automation, and digital literacy coaching.
programs.howToJoin: Register at laworks.com and filter opportunities by 'technology' and 'education'. Many opportunities are one-day or weekend commitments. Age minimum: 14 with parent/guardian.

programs.name: Caltech Summer Tech Camp
programs.desc: A summer program at Caltech for high school students ages 13–18 to explore computer science, AI, and technology. Participants work on hands-on projects with access to Caltech facilities and mentorship from researchers. Multiple tracks including AI/ML, robotics, and data science.
programs.howToJoin: Applications open each winter at summer.caltech.edu. Spots are competitive — apply early and tailor your application to the AI/ML track. Financial aid available.
programs.timeline: Program runs June–August; applications open January–March

talksAndEvents.name: TED AI Conference
talksAndEvents.desc: Annual TED conference dedicated entirely to AI, gathering leading researchers, entrepreneurs, and artists to explore AI's impact on humanity. Past speakers include Andrej Karpathy, Sam Altman, and Yann LeCun.
talksAndEvents.whyAttend: Direct access to cutting-edge ideas from the world's top AI thinkers in an intimate setting — and livestream is free.

talksAndEvents.name: NVIDIA GTC (GPU Technology Conference)
talksAndEvents.desc: NVIDIA's flagship AI and accelerated computing conference. Jensen Huang's keynote is a must-watch for anyone in AI. Hundreds of technical sessions on deep learning, robotics, autonomous vehicles, and more.
talksAndEvents.whyAttend: Free online access to the biggest industry keynotes and technical sessions on AI hardware and applications.

talksAndEvents.name: NeurIPS (Neural Information Processing Systems)
talksAndEvents.desc: The world's most prestigious machine learning research conference. Breakthrough papers in deep learning, reinforcement learning, and AI safety are presented here first. Student volunteer programs offer free attendance.
talksAndEvents.whyAttend: Student volunteer applications open each summer — work 1-2 days in exchange for free full conference access.

talksAndEvents.name: Google I/O
talksAndEvents.desc: Google's annual developer conference showcasing the latest in AI, Android, and cloud. AI keynotes feature announcements from Google DeepMind, Gemini models, and Google Labs — all fully livestreamed for free.
talksAndEvents.whyAttend: Free livestream of major AI product announcements directly from Google engineers and researchers.

talksAndEvents.name: SXSW EDU
talksAndEvents.desc: Where education and innovation meet. AI in education, youth entrepreneurship, and student-led startup showcases. High school students can participate in competitions and pitch events.
talksAndEvents.whyAttend: One of the few major tech conferences specifically welcoming high school students with dedicated programs and competitions.

talksAndEvents.name: Stanford HAI AI Salon
talksAndEvents.desc: Monthly conversations between AI researchers, policymakers, artists, and community leaders on the societal impact of AI. Hosted by Fei-Fei Li and John Etchemendy. Open to public and high school students.
talksAndEvents.whyAttend: Free access to Stanford's AI faculty and guests — held on campus just 30 minutes from LA / accessible via Caltrain.

talksAndEvents.name: CES — AI & Future Tech Showcases
talksAndEvents.desc: The world's largest consumer technology show. AI applications across robotics, smart home, healthcare, mobility and more. Major company keynotes from Samsung, Sony, Nvidia, and startups.
talksAndEvents.whyAttend: Hands-on exposure to AI products before they launch, and direct interaction with engineers and product teams.

talksAndEvents.name: Fortune Brainstorm AI
talksAndEvents.desc: C-suite executives, AI researchers, and policymakers discuss AI's impact on business, society, and government. Past speakers: Sam Altman, Jensen Huang, Satya Nadella, Fei-Fei Li.
talksAndEvents.whyAttend: Free livestream of frank discussions between the most influential decision-makers in AI.

talksAndEvents.name: The AI Summit
talksAndEvents.desc: Practical AI conference focused on real business implementation. Case studies from Fortune 500 companies on deploying AI at scale. Good for understanding how AI is used in industry beyond research.
talksAndEvents.whyAttend: Understand the gap between AI research and production deployment — crucial context for internship and career conversations.
